_comment: Key illustrated events for Chapter 14 — Farewell, Teacher (quiet/short, emotional). Verified spine: Dadoji Konddev died ~1647 and Shivaji (~17) took full charge of the Pune jagir, using his Sanskrit Rajmudra seal (Devanagari, not Persian; 'for the welfare of the people'). Deathbed dialogue is an imagined representative dramatisation (flagged). Dadoji kept neutral and role-focused per sensitivity note. 'anchor' is an EXACT substring of chapters/ch14.md. 'refs' are entity slugs from reference/index.json.

events.title: The pen he could no longer hold
events.anchor: could no longer hold the pen
events.scene_prompt: A quiet, tender domestic interior in the Lal Mahal: the elderly administrator Dadoji Konddev, now frail and white-haired and ill, sits propped among cushions, his trembling old hand having just set down a reed pen over an open ledger/account scroll he is too weak to write in. Shivaji (now a young man of ~17-18) sits close beside him, attentive and gently concerned, ready to read the accounts aloud for him. Soft warm indoor light, hushed and intimate, the sadness of a strong man grown weak. Dadoji matches his reference but visibly older, frailer and unwell. Shivaji now has his developing VAN DYKE beard with SIDEBURNS (a neat, not-yet-full young man's beard, not clean-shaven) and his distinctive white wound turban with the rear peak and the tassel hanging DOWN at the BACK (not sideways), matching his young-man reference. CRUCIAL — render Shivaji's turban (jiretop) shape EXACTLY as in his young-man reference: a white wound pheta that sits LOW and smooth across the forehead at the FRONT and rises to a soft raised PEAK at the upper BACK of the head (the height and bulk are at the BACK), with the pearl-and-cord tassel hanging straight DOWN from that rear peak beside one ear — NOT a round bulbous dome, NOT a tall cone at the front, NOT tilted sideways. Shivaji wears a small RED, UPWARD-pointing crescent-moon mark (a chandrakor tilak) at the centre of his forehead.

events.title: Govern well
events.anchor: Shivaji bent down to hear him
events.scene_prompt: The emotional heart of the chapter, handled gently and without anything gruesome: the dying old teacher Dadoji Konddev lies among cushions in a softly lamplit room of the Lal Mahal, very frail and near the end, and reaches up to speak his last counsel ('Govern well') as Shivaji (now a young man of ~17-18) bends low over him, holding the old man's hand, his own face full of grief and love and resolve, leaning in to catch the faint words. Profoundly tender, quiet, reverent — a deathbed farewell between teacher and student. Warm low lamplight, soft shadows. NOT gory or frightening, just sad and loving. Dadoji matches his reference but aged and frail. Shivaji now has his developing VAN DYKE beard with SIDEBURNS (a neat, not-yet-full young man's beard, not clean-shaven) and his distinctive white wound turban with the rear peak and the tassel hanging DOWN at the BACK (not sideways), matching his young-man reference. CRUCIAL — render Shivaji's turban (jiretop) shape EXACTLY as in his young-man reference: a white wound pheta that sits LOW and smooth across the forehead at the FRONT and rises to a soft raised PEAK at the upper BACK of the head (the height and bulk are at the BACK), with the pearl-and-cord tassel hanging straight DOWN from that rear peak beside one ear — NOT a round bulbous dome, NOT a tall cone at the front, NOT tilted sideways. Shivaji wears a small RED, UPWARD-pointing crescent-moon mark (a chandrakor tilak) at the centre of his forehead.

events.title: A different house
events.scene_prompt: A quiet scene of dignified grief: Shivaji (now a young man of ~17-18) and his mother Jijabai stand together in mourning in the Lal Mahal after the teacher's death — Shivaji grieving openly but with composure, Jijabai beside him with her back straight and her sorrow held quiet behind steady eyes, a comforting hand perhaps on her son's shoulder. Subdued, soft, sorrowful light; an empty cushioned seat or a closed ledger in the background hinting at the absence left behind. Restrained and moving, no bodies shown. Jijabai matches her reference (now a little older); Shivaji now has his developing VAN DYKE beard with SIDEBURNS (a neat, not-yet-full young man's beard, not clean-shaven) and his distinctive white wound turban with the rear peak and the tassel hanging DOWN at the BACK (not sideways), matching his young-man reference. CRUCIAL — render Shivaji's turban (jiretop) shape EXACTLY as in his young-man reference: a white wound pheta that sits LOW and smooth across the forehead at the FRONT and rises to a soft raised PEAK at the upper BACK of the head (the height and bulk are at the BACK), with the pearl-and-cord tassel hanging straight DOWN from that rear peak beside one ear — NOT a round bulbous dome, NOT a tall cone at the front, NOT tilted sideways. BOTH Shivaji and his mother Jijabai wear a small RED, UPWARD-pointing crescent-moon mark (a chandrakor bindi/kunku) at the centre of the forehead. Jijabai here is OLDER (around fifty) and NOT pregnant — a dignified mature woman.

events.title: His own seal
events.anchor: He pressed it into the warm wax and lifted it away
events.scene_prompt: A quietly momentous close-up by warm lamplight in the Lal Mahal: Shivaji (now a young man of about seventeen-eighteen, with his developing VAN DYKE beard and SIDEBURNS) presses his royal seal down onto a folded cream letter / official order on a low desk, and the fresh octagonal seal impression it leaves is clearly visible on the paper. The seal and its impression are the AUTHENTIC octagonal Rajmudra, matching the Rajmudra reference image EXACTLY — an eight-sided seal bearing the real Sanskrit inscription in Devanagari script in horizontal lines (keep the script faithful and legible, do not garble it). His face above is solemn, proud and resolute. CRUCIAL: his white wound turban sits correctly — low across the brow at the front and rising to a peak at the BACK of the head, with the pearl-and-cord tassel hanging straight DOWN at the back/beside one ear, NOT tilted or sideways. Intimate, significant, dignified — a small act of great defiance. Warm lamp glow, focus on the seal, the impression and his face. He matches his young-man reference. CRUCIAL — render Shivaji's turban (jiretop) shape EXACTLY as in his young-man reference: a white wound pheta that sits LOW and smooth across the forehead at the FRONT and rises to a soft raised PEAK at the upper BACK of the head (the height and bulk are at the BACK), with the pearl-and-cord tassel hanging straight DOWN from that rear peak beside one ear — NOT a round bulbous dome, NOT a tall cone at the front, NOT tilted sideways. Shivaji wears a small RED, UPWARD-pointing crescent-moon mark (a chandrakor tilak) at the centre of his forehead.
